roots.title: Midterm Logistics and Gradient Descent Concepts
roots.summary: The midterm is scheduled for October 23rd from 7-9 PM, with a review session on Friday in Evans 10, which has limited seating. Students are instructed on assigned seating and accommodation forms. The lecture introduces gradient descent, including stochastic gradient descent, feature engineering, and DMAIDA models. It reviews the previous lecture's model and loss function, emphasizing the importance of selecting an appropriate model and loss for optimization. An overview of the Gradient Descent algorithm for optimizing models, particularly linear regression, is provided, detailing the selection of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss function and the iterative update process. The update rule is explained, focusing on parameter adjustments based on the gradient of the loss function. The discussion includes one-dimensional and higher-dimensional models, highlighting the complexity with more parameters and the significance of the gradient vector. It covers the gradient of 2D functions, illustrating the calculation of partial derivatives and the gradient as a vector, emphasizing notation and derivation in understanding gradients. Finally, an introduction to using PyTorch for defining loss functions and models in AI is given, highlighting automatic gradient computation and GPU processing efficiency, along with the implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) and its parameters.

children.title: Midterm Logistics and Gradient Descent Concepts
children.summary: The midterm is scheduled for October 23rd from 7-9 PM, with a review session on Friday in Evans 10, which has limited seating. Students are instructed on assigned seating and accommodation forms. The lecture introduces gradient descent, including stochastic gradient descent, feature engineering, and DMAIDA models. It also reviews the previous lecture's model and loss function, highlighting the importance of selecting an appropriate model and loss for optimization.

children.title: Midterm Logistics and Gradient Descent Overview
children.summary: The lecture covers midterm logistics, including the date (October 23rd, 7-9 PM), a review session on Friday in Evans 10 with limited seating, and instructions for assigned seating and accommodation forms for students. It also introduces the conclusion of gradient descent, the concept of stochastic gradient descent, feature engineering, and DMAIDA models, while reviewing the previous lecture's model and loss function, emphasizing the importance of selecting a model and loss for optimization.

children.title: Midterm Logistics and Upcoming Topics
children.summary: The lecture will cover the conclusion of gradient descent and introduce stochastic gradient descent, along with feature engineering and DMAIDA models. The midterm is scheduled for Wednesday, October 23rd, from 7 to 9 PM, with logistics available online. A review session will be held on Friday in Evans 10, with limited seating. Students must check their assigned seating locations for the midterm and fill out accommodation forms if needed. DSP students will receive separate communication regarding accommodations.

children.title: Gradient Descent and Midterm Logistics
children.summary: The segment covers the process of creating descent and introduces multi-dimensional models in the context of optimization. It reviews the previous lecture's model and loss function, emphasizing the recipe for modeling: choosing a model and loss to optimize. Additionally, midterm logistics are announced, including the midterm date (October 23 from 7-9 PM), a review session on Friday in Evans 10, seating assignments to be sent out on October 21, and a reminder for students needing accommodations to fill out a specific form.

children.title: Gradient Descent Algorithm and Gradient Calculation
children.summary: The segment provides an overview of the Gradient Descent algorithm for optimizing models, particularly linear regression with the equation y = θ1x. It details the steps of selecting a loss function, specifically Mean Squared Error (MSE), and minimizing average loss through iterative updates. The update rule is explained, focusing on parameter adjustments based on the gradient of the loss function. The discussion includes one-dimensional and higher-dimensional models, highlighting the complexity with more parameters and the significance of the gradient vector in indicating the optimal direction for loss minimization. It also covers the gradient of 2D functions, using examples to illustrate the calculation of partial derivatives and the gradient as a vector. The importance of notation and derivation in understanding gradients is emphasized, along with the interpretation of gradients in relation to model weights and loss, and the gradient descent update rules in both 1D and 2D.

children.title: Gradient Descent Algorithm and Its Applications
children.summary: The segment provides an overview of the Gradient Descent algorithm used for optimizing models, specifically linear regression represented as y = θ1x. It outlines the steps involved: selecting a loss function, specifically the Mean Squared Error (MSE), and fitting the model by minimizing the average loss through iterative updates. The update rule is explained, highlighting how to adjust parameters based on the gradient of the loss function. The discussion extends to one-dimensional and higher-dimensional models, noting the increased complexity with more parameters. Additionally, the importance of the gradient vector and its role in indicating the optimal direction for minimizing the loss function is emphasized.

children.title: Gradient Descent Algorithm Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses the iterative algorithm known as Gradient Descent used for optimizing models. It outlines the steps involved: first, choosing a single parameter model, specifically a linear regression model represented as y = θ1x; second, selecting the loss function, which is the Mean Squared Error (MSE); and finally, fitting the model by minimizing the average loss using Gradient Descent. The update rule for the parameters is provided, showing how to adjust the model based on the gradient of the loss function.

children.title: Gradient Descent Algorithm Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses the gradient descent algorithm, starting with the concept of moving in the opposite direction of the gradient to minimize a loss function. It emphasizes the importance of the chain rule in calculating the gradient and provides a step-by-step outline of the algorithm: choosing a model, selecting a loss function (Mean Squared Error), and fitting the model by minimizing the average loss using gradient descent. The update rule for the current guess is introduced, where the current guess is adjusted by subtracting a scaled gradient.

"""Performs n steps of gradient descent on df using learning rate alp

Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...

while len(guesses) < t:

guesses. append ( current_guess)
return np. array(guesses)

children.title: Gradient Descent in One and Higher Dimensions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the concept of gradient descent, starting with a one-dimensional model where the goal is to minimize the mean square error by adjusting a single parameter, theta. The process involves moving in the opposite direction of the gradient until reaching a point where the slope is zero, indicating the minimum. The lecture then transitions to higher-dimensional models, explaining that while the basic algorithm remains the same, the complexity increases with more parameters, potentially reaching thousands or even billions of dimensions in modern applications.

children.title: Understanding Gradient Vectors and Derivatives
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of the gradient vector in relation to the loss function, emphasizing that the negative gradient indicates the optimal direction to minimize the loss in a 2D or higher-dimensional surface. It explains how to compute the gradient by taking the partial derivative of the loss with respect to each parameter. An active poll is conducted to assess understanding of gradients through a specific function, prompting participants to compute the gradient for given expressions.

children.title: Gradient of 2D Functions and Calculation Techniques
children.summary: The discussion covers the gradient of 2D functions, starting with the function f(θ_0, θ_1) = 80θ_0^2 + 300θ_1, where participants engage in polling to identify the correct gradient expression. The calculation of the gradient involves determining partial derivatives, treating other variables as constants, and applying the power rule. An example function, f(θ_0, θ_1) = 8θ_0^2 + 3θ_1, is used to illustrate the gradient as a vector of partial derivatives. Further, the gradient of f(x_0, y_0) = 80x_0 + 300y_0 is computed, resulting in ∇f = (16, 36), emphasizing the importance of correct notation and derivation in understanding gradients.

children.title: Gradient of a 2D Function and Polling
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the gradient of a 2D function, specifically f(θ_0, θ_1) = 80θ_0^2 + 300θ_1. The concept of the gradient is introduced, and participants are engaged through active polling to determine the correct expression for the gradient. The options presented in the poll include various formulations involving θ_0 and θ_1, leading to a collaborative exploration of how to derive the gradient for the given function.

children.title: Gradient of a 2D Function Example
children.summary: The segment discusses the gradient of a 2D function, specifically f(x_0, y_0) = 80x_0 + 300y_0. It defines the gradient as ∇f = (∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y) and provides calculations for the partial derivatives, resulting in ∇f = (16, 36). The example illustrates how to compute the gradient for functions of two variables.

An example function f(x_0, y_0) = 80x_0 + 300y_0 is presented, illustrating the calculation of the gradient vector. The gradient is expressed in vector notation, highlighting the components derived from the partial derivatives. This segment emphasizes the importance of correct notation and derivation in understanding gradients.

children.title: Understanding Gradients and Gradient Descent Update Rules
children.summary: The segment covers the interpretation of gradients in relation to model weights and loss, explaining that adjusting model weights affects loss and that the gradient indicates the optimal direction for minimizing loss. It introduces the gradient vector, composed of partial derivatives of loss with respect to each parameter, which points downhill on the loss surface. The discussion includes the gradient descent update rules in both 1D and 2D, presenting the update rule A(t+1) = A(t) - α ∇L(A(t)) for multiple parameters, emphasizing the use of vectors for updates and how the concept extends from one-dimensional to multi-dimensional models.

children.title: Understanding Gradients and Loss Interpretation
children.summary: The segment discusses how to interpret gradients in the context of model weights and loss. It explains that nudging the model weights affects the loss, and the gradient indicates the direction to adjust the weights to minimize loss. The gradient vector is introduced, highlighting that it consists of partial derivatives of loss with respect to each parameter. This vector always points downhill on the loss surface, guiding parameter adjustments for optimization.

children.title: Gradient Descent Update Rules in 1D and 2D
children.summary: The segment discusses the gradient vector in the context of parameter values for a two-parameter model, emphasizing the calculation of the partial derivative of loss with respect to each parameter. It introduces the concept of the gradient vector, which indicates the downhill direction on the loss surface. The lecture recalls the 1D update rule for gradient descent, A(+1) = A(t) - α ∇L(A(t)), and extends this to models with multiple parameters, presenting the update in vector form for gradient descent in multiple dimensions.

children.title: Gradient Descent Update Rule in Multiple Dimensions
children.summary: The segment discusses the update rule for gradient descent in multiple dimensions, building on the 1D update rule. The formula for updating parameters is presented as A(t+1) = A(t) - α ∇L(A(t)), where A represents the parameters, α is the learning rate, and ∇L is the gradient of the loss function. The focus is on how this rule is adapted for models with multiple parameters, emphasizing the use of vectors for the updates.

children.title: Gradient Descent Update Rule in Multiple Dimensions
children.summary: The update rule for gradient descent in one dimension is presented as A(t+1) = A(t) - α∇L(A(t)). For models with multiple parameters, the update is expressed in vector form, emphasizing the transition from one-dimensional to multi-dimensional gradient descent, allowing for a more compact representation of the update process.

children.title: Gradient Descent and Loss Functions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on optimizing a loss function, typically represented as an average, with a specific example using a squared loss for a 2-dimensional model. The gradient descent update rule is introduced, emphasizing the adjustment of parameters in the negative direction of the gradient until convergence is achieved. The segment covers the mathematical formulation for updating parameters in multiple dimensions, illustrating the process of calculating gradients and applying them to update the model parameters.

children.title: Gradient Descent Algorithm and Update Rule
children.summary: The gradient descent algorithm is explained as a method to adjust parameters in the negative direction of the gradient until convergence is achieved. The update rule is defined as θ(t+1) = θ(t) - α∇L(θ(t)), where ∇L is the gradient of the loss function evaluated at the current parameters. The model is presented as f(x) = x^Tθ, with a loss function defined as l(y, g) = (y - g)^2. Specific calculations for the gradients of the loss function with respect to the parameters are provided, illustrating how to compute the necessary updates for each data point.

children.title: Stopping Conditions for Gradient Descent
children.summary: Two common stopping conditions for the gradient descent algorithm are discussed: running the iterative procedure for a fixed number of steps, such as 10,000, or halting when the updates to the parameters become very small, indicating convergence. The gradient descent update rule is presented, emphasizing the need to nudge the parameters in the negative gradient direction until convergence is achieved. The loss function and its gradient are also outlined, demonstrating how updates are calculated based on the model's predictions.

children.title: Gradient Descent and Multi-Dimensional Models
children.summary: The gradient descent algorithm updates parameters by nudging them in the negative direction of the gradient of the loss function until convergence is achieved. Convergence occurs when updates no longer significantly change the parameters. The discussion then shifts to applying gradient descent to multi-dimensional models, specifically for predicting tips based on total bill and a constant offset. The model is expressed as tip = β0 + β1 * bill, requiring optimization of both parameters. A bias term is added to the data to represent the intercept, and the dataset is prepared for analysis.

Gradient Descent on Multi-Dimensional Models
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
Now suppose we improve our model so that we want to predict the tip from the total_bill plus a constant offset, in other words:
tip = 00+01bill
Our simple linear regression model has two parameters, 00 and 01. We need to optimize both of them.
Fortunately, gradient descent readily extends to models with multiple dimenions.
Defining a 2D MSE Function
Now, we can give our predictions as
§ = 00 + 01x1
In the cell below, we add a bias term to our data to represent the constant intercept 00.
[ ]:
tips_with_bias = df.copy( )
tips_with_bias ["bias"] = 1
tips_with_bias = tips_with_bias [ ["bias", "total_bill"] ]
tips_with_bias.head( )
[ ]:
X = tips_with_bias
y = df ["tip"]
Throughout this problem, we'll assume we want to minimize the mean squared error of our predictions, i.e.
[ ]: def mse_loss (theta):
y_hat = X @ theta
return np.mean((y - y_hat) ** 2)
Using this function, we can visualize our loss function. Because we now want to understand how the loss changes with respect to two parameters, we create a loss surface. Each point on the surface
represents the loss of the model for a particular choice of 00 and 01.
The cell below uses lots of syntax you've never seen. No need to worry about any unfamiliar plotting code; for now, focus on the output.

children.title: Modeling Tips with Loss Function Visualization
children.summary: The segment discusses a predictive model for tip percentages using a dataset. It introduces two parameters: theta zero (intercept) and theta one (slope). The goal is to minimize the loss function, visualized as a surface where each point represents the loss for a specific model choice. A heat map illustrates the contours of loss, indicating that brighter colors represent higher loss and darker colors represent lower loss. The discussion also touches on the challenges of visualizing high-dimensional data and mentions derived gradients for the model parameters.

import plotly. graph_objects as go
uvalues = np. linspace(-1, 5, 20)
vvalues = np. linspace(-0.1, 0.35, 20)
(u,v) = np.meshgrid(uvalues, vvalues)
thetas = np.vstack((u.flatten( ), v. flatten()))
MSE = np.array( [mse_loss(t) for t in thetas.T] )
loss_surface = go. Surface (x=u,
y=v, z=np. reshape (MSE, u. shape),
contours = {"z": {"show": True, "start": 0, "end": 50, "size": 2, "color": "white"}} )
# This is an approximate guess for the optimal point.
ind = np.argmin (MSE)
optimal_point = go. Scatter3d(name = "Optimal Point",
x = [thetas. T [ind, 0]], y = [thetas. T [ind, 1] ] ,
z = [MSE [ind] ] ,
marker=dict(size=10, color="red") )
fig = go. Figure(data=[loss_surface, optimal_point] )
fig. update_layout (scene = dict(
xaxis_title = "theta0",
yaxis_title = "theta1",
zaxis_title = "MSE"), autosize=False, width=800, height=600)
fig. show( )

When working with multidimensional models, we optimize a vector of parameters. Our new update rule is:
@(+1) = 0(+) - aV;L(@(*)
Where V -L(0(t)) is the gradient of the loss function. It is the vector of the partial derivatives of loss with respect to each parameter 0 ;.

[ ]: def mse_gradient (theta, X, y):
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
x0 = X. iloc[ :, 0]
x1 = X. iloc[ :, 1]
dth0 = np.mean(-2 * (y - theta[0]*x0 - theta[1]*x1) * x0)
dth1 = np.mean(-2 * (y - theta[0]*x0 - theta[1]*x1) *x1)
return np. array ( [dth0, dth1])
[ ]: def sgd(grad, X, y, initial_theta, eta = 0.3, max_iter = 5000, batch_size=50 ):
theta = initial_theta
thetas = [theta]
n = len (X)
for t in range(1, max_iter) :
X_sample = X.sample(batch_size)
y_sample = y. loc [X_sample. index]
theta = theta - eta/t * grad(theta, X_sample, y_sample)
thetas. append (theta)
return thetas

import plotly. graph_objects as go
uvalues = np. linspace(-1, 5, 20)
vvalues = np. linspace(-0.1, 0.35, 20)
(u,v) = np.meshgrid(uvalues, vvalues)
thetas = np.vstack((u.flatten( ), v. flatten()))
MSE = np.array( [mse_loss(t) for t in thetas.T] )
loss_surface = go. Surface (x=u,
y=v, z=np. reshape (MSE, u. shape),
contours = {"z": {"show": True, "start": 0, "end": 50, "size": 2, "color": "white"}} )
# This is an approximate guess for the optimal point.
ind = np.argmin (MSE)
optimal_point = go. Scatter3d(name = "Optimal Point",
x = [thetas. T [ind, 0]], y = [thetas. T [ind, 1] ] ,
z = [MSE [ind] ] ,
marker=dict(size=10, color="red") )
fig = go. Figure(data=[loss_surface, optimal_point] )
fig. update_layout (scene = dict(
xaxis_title = "theta0",
yaxis_title = "theta1",
zaxis_title = "MSE"), autosize=False, width=800, height=600)
fig. show( )

[ ]: def mse_gradient (theta, X, y):
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
x0 = X. iloc[ :, 0]
x1 = X. iloc[ :, 1]
dth0 = np.mean(-2 * (y - theta[0]*x0 - theta[1]*x1) * x0)
dth1 = np.mean(-2 * (y - theta[0]*x0 - theta[1]*x1) *x1)
return np. array ( [dth0, dth1])

n = len (X)
for t in range(1, max_iter) :
X_sample = X.sample(batch_size)
y_sample = y. loc [X_sample. index]
theta = theta - eta/t * grad(theta, X_sample, y_sample)
thetas. append (theta)
return thetas

import plotly. graph_objects as go
uvalues = np. linspace(-1, 5, 20)
vvalues = np. linspace(-0.1, 0.35, 20)
(u,v) = np.meshgrid(uvalues, vvalues)
thetas = np.vstack((u.flatten( ), v. flatten()))
MSE = np.array( [mse_loss(t) for t in thetas.T] )
loss_surface = go. Surface (x=u,
y=v, z=np. reshape (MSE, u. shape),
contours = {"z": {"show": True, "start": 0, "end": 50, "size": 2, "color": "white"}} )
# This is an approximate guess for the optimal point.
ind = np.argmin (MSE)
optimal_point = go. Scatter3d(name = "Optimal Point",
x = [thetas. T [ind, 0]], y = [thetas. T [ind, 1] ] ,
z = [MSE [ind] ] ,
marker=dict(size=10, color="red") )
fig = go. Figure(data=[loss_surface, optimal_point] )
fig. update_layout (scene = dict(
xaxis_title = "theta0",
yaxis_title = "theta1",
zaxis_title = "MSE"), autosize=False, width=800, height=600)
fig. show( )

import plotly. graph_objects as go
uvalues = np. linspace(-1, 5, 20)
vvalues = np. linspace(-0.1, 0.35, 20)
(u,v) = np.meshgrid(uvalues, vvalues)
thetas = np.vstack((u.flatten( ), v. flatten()))
MSE = np.array( [mse_loss(t) for t in thetas.T] )
loss_surface = go. Surface (x=u,
y=v, z=np. reshape (MSE, u. shape),
contours = {"z": {"show": True, "start": 0, "end": 50, "size": 2, "color": "white"}} )
# This is an approximate guess for the optimal point.
ind = np.argmin (MSE)
optimal_point = go. Scatter3d(name = "Optimal Point",
x = [thetas. T [ind, 0]], y = [thetas. T [ind, 1] ] ,
z = [MSE [ind] ] ,
marker=dict(size=10, color="red") )
fig = go. Figure(data=[loss_surface, optimal_point] )
fig. update_layout (scene = dict(
xaxis_title = "theta0",
yaxis_title = "theta1",
zaxis_title = "MSE"), autosize=False, width=800, height=600)
fig. show( )

When working with multidimensional models, we optimize a vector of parameters. Our new update rule is:
@(+1) = 0(+) - aV;L(@(*)
Where V -L(0(t)) is the gradient of the loss function. It is the vector of the partial derivatives of loss with respect to each parameter 0 ;.
Let's derive the gradient of the loss for our problem:

[52]:
def mse_gradient (theta):
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
#x0 = X.iloc[ :, 0] constant term (1)
x1 = X. iloc[ :, 1]
dth0 = np.mean(32 * (y - (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)))
dth1 = np.mean(-2* (y- (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)) *x1)
return np. array ( [dth0, dth1])
Now, we can use our gradient_descent function from before to optimize 00 and 01 at the same time! The final estimates for the ideal model parameters are very similar to the guesses we may have made
simply by inspecting the plot of the loss surface from before.
The cell below may take a moment to run.
[ ]:
guesses = gradient_descent (mse_gradient, np.array( [1, . 5]), 0.001, 10000)
pd. DataFrame (guesses, columns=["theta_0", "theta_1"] ).tail (10)
Comparing with the scipy minimize function.
[ ]: minimize(mse_loss, x0 = [0,0])
We can actually provide the gradient information to the scipy optimizer to get an even faster solution.
[ ]: minimize(mse_loss, x0 = [0,0],jac=mse_gradient)

def mse_gradient (theta):
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
#x0 = X.iloc[ :, 0] constant term (1)

dth0 = np.mean(-2 * (y - (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)) * 1)
dth1 = np.mean(-2* (y - (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)) * x1)
return np. array ( [dth0, dth1])
Now, we can use our gradient_descent function from before to optimize 00 and 01 at the same time! The final estimates for the ideal model parameters are very similar to the guesses we may have made
simply by inspecting the plot of the loss surface from before.

children.title: Introduction to Stochastic Gradient Descent and Computational Complexity
children.summary: This segment introduces Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) as a crucial algorithm for 2024, highlighting its significance and excitement surrounding the topic. It revisits gradient descent, explaining the calculation of the average gradient of loss across data points and discussing various forms, including batch, mini-batch, and stochastic methods. The computational complexity of gradient descent is examined, noting that the computation requires at least n times d work for n entries and D-dimensional vectors, especially when iterating T times. To enhance efficiency, the concept of sampling is introduced, allowing for the use of a probability sample to approximate the gradient and reduce computational demands. The segment further explains how to compute an average over a sample of B data points to approximate the true gradient, emphasizing the importance of sampling in this process.

children.title: Introduction to Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: The segment introduces stochastic gradient descent as a key algorithm for 2024, emphasizing its relevance and encouraging excitement about the topic. It briefly revisits gradient descent, explaining that it involves calculating the average gradient of loss across data points. The discussion touches on various forms of gradient descent, including batch, mini-batch, and stochastic methods, setting the stage for deeper exploration of these concepts in the lecture.

children.title: Computational Complexity of Gradient Descent
children.summary: The segment discusses the computational complexity involved in gradient descent, highlighting that for n entries and D-dimensional vectors, the computation requires at least n times d work. With examples of large values for n (10 million) and D (100,000), it emphasizes the extensive computation needed, especially when iterating T times. To improve efficiency, the concept of sampling is introduced, suggesting that rather than using all data points, a probability sample can be used to approximate the gradient, thereby reducing the computational burden.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Approximating the Gradient
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of approximating the gradient using a probability sample in Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD). It explains how to compute an average over a sample of B data points to obtain an approximation of the true gradient. The speaker illustrates this with examples of sample indices and emphasizes the importance of sampling in the gradient computation process.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Approximating the Gradient
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is introduced as a method for approximating the true gradient using a probability sample. The true gradient is defined mathematically, and the concept of sampling records to estimate the gradient is emphasized.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Overview
children.summary: The original stochastic gradient descent (SGD) algorithm involves taking one data point from the dataset to determine the direction for minimizing loss. By evaluating the gradient based on this single data point, a small step is taken in that direction. This process is repeated with different random data points, leading to a series of small adjustments that ideally converge towards the minimum loss. The concept of using a probability sample to approximate the gradient is introduced, with the mini-batch size typically being small, and the original SGD algorithm being characterized by a mini-batch size of one.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Sampling the Gradient
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is introduced as a method for approximating the gradient using a probability sample. The true gradient is defined as the average of the evaluations of the function over all data points. To reduce noise in the sampling process, a mini-batch size, typically small, is employed. The original SGD algorithm operates with a mini-batch size of one, allowing for more efficient gradient estimation by sampling a subset of records.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent and Batch Size Effects
children.summary: The choice of batch size (B) in Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) influences the accuracy of gradient estimates and computational efficiency. A larger batch size provides a better estimate of the curvature of the descent surface, allowing for more consistent direction in optimization. However, increasing B also raises computational demands, which can be addressed through parallel processing on GPUs. While larger samples improve gradient estimates, diminishing returns occur after a certain point, suggesting that a smaller batch size may allow for more frequent updates and potentially better overall performance.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Implementation and Overview
children.summary: The segment covers the implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) in Python, detailing how to estimate the true gradient of the loss surface using data samples for faster computation. It discusses the initialization of theta, the algorithm's execution over multiple epochs, and the importance of shuffling data to maintain the relationship between input features (X) and output labels (Y). The process involves using mini-batches to compute gradients efficiently, with an update rule for theta based on the learning rate and gradients from the mini-batch. An example with a batch size of 10 illustrates the iterative updating process.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Implementation in Python
children.summary: The segment discusses a short Python implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD). It explains the process of estimating the true gradient of the loss surface using a sample of data, which allows for quicker computation of gradients. The code outlines the initialization of theta, running the algorithm for a specified number of epochs, optionally shuffling the data, and updating theta using mini-batches.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Process Overview
children.summary: The process of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) involves running multiple epochs over the entire dataset, where data is shuffled to maintain the relationship between input features (X) and output labels (Y). The algorithm computes gradients more quickly by using mini-batches of data, ensuring that the rows and columns of X and Y are shuffled together to avoid disrupting their correspondence. The provided pseudocode outlines the iterative process of updating the model parameters (theta) based on the computed gradients from each mini-batch.

children.title: Stochastic Mini-Batch Gradient Descent Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses stochastic mini-batch gradient descent, which estimates the true gradient of the loss surface using a small sample of data. It introduces the update rule for the parameter theta, defined as the old theta minus the product of the learning rate alpha and the gradient computed from a mini-batch of data. An example is given where a batch size B is set to 10, illustrating the process of running through epochs and updating theta based on the gradients of the selected mini-batch.

The process involves shuffling the data each time to ensure randomness in sampling, which helps in estimating the true gradient of the loss surface. The segment includes a brief code outline for implementing SGD, highlighting the initialization of parameters, the shuffling of data, and the iterative updates of the model parameters (theta) through epochs and mini-batches.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Algorithm Overview
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is introduced as a method to estimate the true gradient of the loss surface using a sample of data, which allows for quicker computation of gradients. The algorithm involves running for K epochs, where the data can be shuffled optionally. The update rule is given as theta = theta - alpha * grad(X[i:(i+b), :], y[i:(i+b)]), where b is the mini-batch size.

children.title: Importance of Shuffling Data for SGD
children.summary: Shuffling data before performing stochastic gradient descent (SGD) is crucial for efficient linear traversal and to prevent computational delays from accessing a large dataset repeatedly. While some practitioners shuffle data once and loop through it multiple times, skipping shuffling can be risky, especially if the data is sorted in a way that affects randomness. Although shuffling can assist in the process, it does not significantly enhance convergence and may increase processing time, so it should be applied judiciously.

children.title: Shuffling Data for Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: Shuffling data before performing stochastic gradient descent is important to ensure efficient linear traversal and avoid computational delays from repeatedly accessing a large dataset. While some practitioners shuffle their data once and loop through it multiple times, others may skip shuffling, which can be risky if the data is sorted in a way that affects randomness. Although shuffling can help, it does not significantly improve convergence and can add processing time, so it should be done judiciously.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Concepts and Steps
children.summary: The segment explains stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and contrasts it with traditional gradient descent. In gradient descent, the algorithm minimizes the loss function by considering all data points, resulting in one gradient update per epoch. In contrast, SGD updates the gradient using one data point at a time, allowing for multiple updates per epoch. A worked example with an ordinary least squares model fitted to a dataset of 8,192 points illustrates the frequency of gradient updates between the two methods. The process of computing gradients with SGD is outlined, focusing on using pairs of data points to approximate the true gradient, emphasizing the iterative nature of taking steps by calculating gradients for additional pairs.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Explained
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and contrasts it with traditional gradient descent. It emphasizes that in gradient descent, the algorithm carefully determines the direction to minimize the loss function by considering all data points, leading to one gradient update per epoch. In contrast, SGD utilizes a random sampling approach, where it updates the gradient using one data point at a time, resulting in multiple updates per epoch. A worked example is presented involving an ordinary least squares model fitted to a dataset of 8,192 points, illustrating the difference in gradient update frequency between the two methods.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Steps Explained
children.summary: The process of computing gradients using stochastic gradient descent is outlined, focusing on using two data points to calculate the gradient. The average of these gradients is taken to approximate the true gradient at the current position. The speaker mentions having 8,192 data points initially, then explains the iterative process of taking steps by computing gradients for additional pairs of data points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm going to compute two gradients or I got to compute the gradient on two data points. Average them. Take one step. That's one step. I've used two data points in my epoch. I have 8,092 minus 2 or 8,190. Data points left. I'll take two more. Construct the gradient for each of those averages. Gradients that will give me the gradient I'm going to take where I'm standing now. I'll take another step. And I'll grab two more data points. Compute the gradient on those data points. You'll average those gradients to get the gradient at this point for two data points, an approximation of the true gradient. Video: 1: : Active poll

children.title: Impact of Batch Size on Gradient Updates in OLS
children.summary: An active poll was conducted regarding the number of gradient updates per epoch when fitting an Ordinary Least Squares model to 8192 datapoints using stochastic gradient descent with a batch size of 1. Smaller batch sizes lead to more frequent gradient updates but result in higher variability in gradient estimates, while larger batch sizes yield more consistent updates but occur less frequently due to increased computational requirements. Choosing an appropriate batch size is crucial for optimizing hardware efficiency and computational performance.

children.title: Impact of Batch Size on Gradient Updates
children.summary: Smaller batch sizes lead to more frequent gradient updates but result in higher variability in the gradient estimates, causing fluctuations. In contrast, larger batch sizes yield more consistent and higher quality gradient updates, though they occur less frequently due to increased computational requirements. An active poll is conducted regarding the number of gradient updates per epoch when using stochastic gradient descent with a batch size of 1 on a dataset of 8192 datapoints.

children.title: Batch Size Tradeoffs in Machine Learning
children.summary: Choosing an appropriate batch size is crucial for hardware efficiency and computational performance. Smaller batch sizes allow for more frequent gradient updates but introduce greater variability in gradient estimates. In contrast, larger batch sizes can leverage hardware acceleration for improved system performance and provide higher quality gradient updates, though they result in less frequent updates. A typical approach is to set the batch size to optimize hardware utilization.

children.title: Introduction to PyTorch and Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: An overview of using PyTorch for defining loss functions and models in AI, highlighting its automatic gradient computation and GPU processing efficiency. The implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is discussed, including parameters like learning rate and batch size, with a focus on the gradient of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) and iterative model parameter updates. A learning rate strategy to prevent overshooting during optimization is introduced. The segment emphasizes the importance of SGD in training large neural networks and generative AI, followed by a midterm review and a suggestion for a brief pause before continuing to feature engineering.

children.title: Introduction to PyTorch and Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: An overview of using PyTorch for defining loss functions and models in AI, highlighting its automatic gradient computation capabilities and the efficiency of GPU processing. The implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is discussed, including a demonstration with parameters like learning rate and batch size. The segment also covers the implementation of SGD for multi-dimensional models, focusing on the gradient of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) and includes functions for computing the MSE gradient and updating model parameters iteratively. A sophisticated learning rate strategy is introduced to prevent overshooting during optimization. Additionally, a midterm review is included, emphasizing the importance of stochastic gradient descent in training large neural networks and generative AI, along with a suggestion for a brief pause before continuing to feature engineering.

children.title: Introduction to PyTorch and Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: An overview of using PyTorch for defining loss functions and models in AI, highlighting its automatic gradient computation capabilities. The discussion emphasizes the efficiency of GPU processing and the simplicity of implementing gradient descent, specifically Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD). A brief mention of past teaching attempts and an invitation to review related slides is included, followed by a demonstration of gradient descent with specific parameters such as learning rate and batch size.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Implementation
children.summary: The segment discusses the implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) for multi-dimensional models, focusing on the gradient of the Mean Squared Error (MSE). It includes a function to compute the MSE gradient based on the input data and parameters, followed by a function for SGD that updates the model parameters iteratively using sampled data. The example demonstrates the application of these functions with specific parameters and outputs the last five values of the parameter array.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent and Midterm Review
children.summary: The discussion covers the process of sampling Y values corresponding to X values using indices, emphasizing the importance of matching these samples. A more sophisticated learning rate strategy is introduced, where the learning rate decreases with each step to prevent overshooting during optimization. The segment concludes with a summary of the material covered for the midterm, indicating that students should review assignments as well. It highlights that stochastic gradient descent is a key algorithm used in training large neural networks and generative AI. A brief pause for stretching is suggested before moving on to feature engineering, with a note to clarify confusion about the concept of epochs.

children.title: Sampling Techniques in Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the importance of sampling in stochastic gradient descent (SGD) to eliminate biases. It emphasizes the need to shuffle data to achieve simple random samples, which helps avoid issues like having similar data points clustered together. This clustering can lead to inefficiencies in the optimization process, causing the algorithm to oscillate between directions. By taking random samples, the algorithm can make progress towards convergence, and under certain conditions, the sampling process can lead to optimal results. The segment also highlights that using multiple steps with a good estimate improves the accuracy of the gradient calculations.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent and Feature Engineering
children.summary: The segment discusses stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and its application in high-dimensional models, emphasizing the importance of small steps for faster convergence. It explains that large neural networks can be viewed as linear models and highlights the significance of transforming data to establish linear relationships. Feature engineering is introduced as a method to enhance model performance through transformations like logarithmic or polynomial adjustments. The concept of feature functions is explained, detailing how they transform raw features into more informative ones, often increasing dataset dimensionality. An example illustrates adding a squared feature to the design matrix, showcasing transformations of features such as horsepower and miles per gallon.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient and Feature Engineering in Models
children.summary: The discussion covers the concept of stochastic gradient descent and its application in large models with high dimensions and numerous tokens, emphasizing the importance of taking small steps for faster convergence. It highlights that even large neural networks can be viewed as linear models, and the key to effective model building lies in transforming data to establish linear relationships. Feature engineering is introduced as a method to enhance model performance by applying transformations to features, such as logarithmic or polynomial adjustments, to align data along a linear path. The focus of the course will be primarily on linear models, which serve as the foundation for many machine learning tasks.

children.title: Feature Engineering and Feature Functions
children.summary: Feature engineering involves transforming raw features into more informative features for modeling, allowing for the capture of domain knowledge, expression of non-linear relationships with linear models, and inclusion of non-numeric features. A feature function describes the specific transformations applied to raw features, often resulting in an increase in the dimension of the dataset. An example provided illustrates a feature function that adds a squared feature to the design matrix.

children.title: Feature Functions and Transformations in Data
children.summary: Feature functions are transformations applied to raw features in a dataset, which often increase the dimensionality of the featurized dataset. An example is provided where a squared feature is added to the design matrix, illustrating how raw features like horsepower (hp) and miles per gallon (mpg) can be transformed into new features such as hp squared. The segment discusses the notation for transformed features, indicating that the original matrix can expand into multiple columns through various transformations, including nonlinear functions such as squares, logs, and products of features.

children.title: Encoding Categorical Features and Regression Techniques
children.summary: Categorical features must be encoded for regression models, typically using one-hot encoding, which creates binary columns for each category. This method transforms non-numeric data, such as a 'region' column, into multiple binary columns indicating presence or absence. Feature functions describe transformations that can increase dataset dimensionality. The '@' symbol is used in linear models to represent the design matrix after feature engineering. The OneHotEncoder class from sklearn is essential for converting non-numeric data, like days of the week, into a suitable format for model fitting, with Fridays and Sundays showing higher predictive power for tips. An example demonstrates the OneHotEncoder's function, converting the 'day' feature into four numeric features. Additionally, the scikit-learn Pipeline class allows for creating feature engineering pipelines that combine transformations and models, enhancing model expressiveness and simplifying debugging, exemplified by using ColumnTransformer for one-hot encoding and polynomial feature generation followed by Linear Regression.

children.title: Encoding Categorical Features for Regression
children.summary: Categorical features must be encoded to include non-numeric data in regression models, typically using one-hot encoding. This method creates binary columns for each category, indicating presence with '1' and absence with '0'. For instance, a 'region' column can be transformed into multiple columns for each region. Feature functions describe the transformations applied to raw features, which can increase dataset dimensionality. Linear models may use the '@' symbol to represent the design matrix post-feature engineering. One-hot encoding effectively converts qualitative data into numeric features, allowing for proper modeling.

children.title: Encoding Categorical Features for Regression
children.summary: To incorporate non-numeric or qualitative features into regression models, categorical features need to be encoded. This involves creating new binary columns for each category that the feature can take, known as one-hot encoding. For example, a 'region' column can be transformed into multiple columns, each representing a specific region with a '1' indicating the presence of that category and '0' elsewhere. Additionally, feature functions describe the transformations applied to raw features, often increasing the dimensionality of the dataset. Linear models trained on this transformed data may use the symbol '@' to denote the design matrix after feature engineering.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding in Feature Engineering
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a technique used in feature engineering to convert qualitative data into numeric features suitable for modeling. Each category of a categorical variable is represented by its own feature, where the value is set to 1 if a row belongs to that category and 0 otherwise. For example, in a dataset with days of the week, 'Sunday', 'Thursday', and 'Saturday' would each have a dedicated feature, allowing for effective numerical representation of categorical data.

children.title: One-hot Encoding in Feature Engineering
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a feature engineering technique that transforms qualitative data into numeric features suitable for modeling. Each category of a categorical variable is assigned its own feature, where the value is 1 if a row belongs to that category and 0 otherwise. For example, in a dataset with categories like Sunday, Thursday, and Saturday, the original data can be represented in a one-hot encoded format, allowing for effective modeling.

children.title: One-hot Encoding in Feature Engineering
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a technique used in feature engineering to convert qualitative data into numeric features suitable for modeling. Each category of a categorical variable is assigned its own feature, where the value is 1 if a row belongs to that category and 0 otherwise. For example, in the original data with days of the week, 'Sunday' would be represented as 1 for 'Sunday' and 0 for other days, while 'Thursday' would be represented as 0 for 'Sunday' and 1 for 'Thursday'.

children.title: One-hot Encoding in Feature Engineering
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a technique used in feature engineering to convert qualitative data into numeric features suitable for modeling. Each category of a categorical variable is assigned its own feature, where the value is set to 1 if a row belongs to that category and 0 otherwise. For example, in the original data with days of the week (Sunday, Thursday, Saturday), one-hot encoding transforms it into a binary format, creating separate columns for each day.

children.summary: One-hot encoding is a feature engineering technique that transforms qualitative data into numeric features suitable for modeling. Each category of a categorical variable is represented by its own feature, where the value is 1 if a row belongs to that category and 0 otherwise. This method helps to encode nominal data without prioritizing any feature, making it a powerful technique for assignments and projects. An example is provided showing how to create binary columns for each unique value in a dataset, such as days of the week.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding in Regression Models
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a powerful technique used to convert categorical variables into a format suitable for regression models. In this segment, the focus is on encoding the 'day of the week' feature from tips data, which will be relevant for assignments. The one-hot encoded features will be integrated into the design matrix for model training, allowing for coefficients to be assigned to each feature, including total bill and size.

It turns out that the examples we looked at above represent a somewhat artificial scenario: we trained our model on all the data we had available, then used the model to make pre Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
dataset. A more realistic situation is when we wish to apply our model on unseen data - that is, datapoints that it did not encounter during the model fitting process.
Suppose we obtain a random sample of 6 datapoints from our population of vehicle data. We want to train a model on these 6 points and use it to make predictions on unseen data (perhaps cars for which
we don't already know the true mpg ).
[*]:
np. random. seed (100)
sample_6 = vehicles.sample(6)
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg")
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
If we design a model with polynomial degree 5, we can make perfect predictions on this sample of training data.
[*]:
degree_5_model = Pipeline( [
('polynomial_transformation', PolynomialFeatures(5)),
('linear_regression', LinearRegression( ) )
degree_5_model. fit(sample_6[["hp"] ], sample_6 ["mpg"] )
xs = np. linspace(0, 250, 1000)
degree_5_model_predictions = degree_5_model.predict(xs[ :, np.newaxis] )
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
However, when we reapply this fitted model to the full population of data, it fails to capture the major trends of the dataset.
[*] :
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=vehicles, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50);
The model has overfit to the data used to train it. It has essentially "memorized" the six datapoints used during model fitting, and does not generalize well to new data.

It turns out that the examples we looked at above represent a somewhat artificial scenario: we trained our model on all the data we had available, then used the model to make pre Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
dataset. A more realistic situation is when we wish to apply our model on unseen data - that is, datapoints that it did not encounter during the model fitting process.
Suppose we obtain a random sample of 6 datapoints from our population of vehicle data. We want to train a model on these 6 points and use it to make predictions on unseen data (perhaps cars for which
we don't already know the true mpg ).
[*]:
np. random. seed (100)
sample_6 = vehicles.sample(6)
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg")
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
If we design a model with polynomial degree 5, we can make perfect predictions on this sample of training data.
[*]:
degree_5_model = Pipeline( [
('polynomial_transformation', PolynomialFeatures(5)),
('linear_regression', LinearRegression( ) )
degree_5_model. fit(sample_6[["hp"] ], sample_6 ["mpg"] )
xs = np. linspace(0, 250, 1000)
degree_5_model_predictions = degree_5_model.predict(xs[ :, np.newaxis] )
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
However, when we reapply this fitted model to the full population of data, it fails to capture the major trends of the dataset.
[*] :
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=vehicles, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50);
The model has overfit to the data used to train it. It has essentially "memorized" the six datapoints used during model fitting, and does not generalize well to new data.

It turns out that the examples we looked at above represent a somewhat artificial scenario: we trained our model on all the data we had available, then used the model to make pre Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
dataset. A more realistic situation is when we wish to apply our model on unseen data - that is, datapoints that it did not encounter during the model fitting process.
Suppose we obtain a random sample of 6 datapoints from our population of vehicle data. We want to train a model on these 6 points and use it to make predictions on unseen data (perhaps cars for which
we don't already know the true mpg ).
[*]:
np. random. seed (100)
sample_6 = vehicles.sample(6)
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg")
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
If we design a model with polynomial degree 5, we can make perfect predictions on this sample of training data.
[*]:
degree_5_model = Pipeline( [
('polynomial_transformation', PolynomialFeatures(5)),
('linear_regression', LinearRegression( ) )
degree_5_model. fit(sample_6[["hp"] ], sample_6 ["mpg"] )
xs = np. linspace(0, 250, 1000)
degree_5_model_predictions = degree_5_model.predict(xs[ :, np.newaxis] )
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
However, when we reapply this fitted model to the full population of data, it fails to capture the major trends of the dataset.
[*] :
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=vehicles, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50);
The model has overfit to the data used to train it. It has essentially "memorized" the six datapoints used during model fitting, and does not generalize well to new data.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding in Feature Engineering
children.summary: One-hot encoding is introduced as a feature engineering technique to convert categorical data into numeric features for modeling. The process involves identifying unique values in categorical variables, such as the day of the week, to create binary columns representing each category. An example is provided using the tips dataset, where one-hot encoding is applied to the 'day' variable, allowing it to be used alongside numeric features like 'total_bill' and 'size' to predict the 'tip'.

Suppose we want to use a design matrix of three features - the total_bill, size , and day - to predict the tip.
[3]:
X_raw = tips [["total_bill", "size", "day"] ]
y = tips ["tip"]
Because day is non-numeric, we will apply one-hot encoding before fitting a model.
The OneHotEncoder class of sklearn (documentation) offers a quick way to perform one-hot encoding. You will explore its use in detail in Lab 7. For now, recognize that we follow a very similar
workflow to when we were working with the LinearRegression class: we initialize a OneHotEncoder object, fit it to our data, then use . transform to apply the fitted encoder.
[4]:
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder
# Initialize a OneHotEncoder object
ohe = OneHotEncoder ( )
# Fit the encoder
ohe. fit (tips [ ["day"] ] )
ohe

children.title: OneHotEncoder Usage in Data Processing
children.summary: The OneHotEncoder class from the sklearn library provides a straightforward method for one-hot encoding categorical variables. The process involves initializing a OneHotEncoder object, fitting it to the data, and then using the .transform method to apply the encoding. An example is provided where the encoder is fitted to the 'day' feature of a dataset, followed by transforming it to produce a matrix representation of the encoded data.

children.title: OneHotEncoder Usage in Data Preprocessing
children.summary: The OneHotEncoder class from scikit-learn simplifies one-hot encoding. The process involves initializing a OneHotEncoder object, fitting it to the data, and then transforming the data using the fitted encoder. An example demonstrates how to fit the encoder to a 'day' feature from a dataset and transform it into an encoded array, showing the first five rows of the resulting matrix.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding and Handling Unknown Categories
children.summary: The segment discusses one-hot encoding, specifically how to encode categorical variables like days of the week using the OneHotEncoder in Python. It explains the concept of one-hot encoding, where each category is represented as a binary vector, with one 'hot' (1) value indicating the presence of that category. An example is provided where a 'fun day' value is introduced, illustrating the issue of encountering unknown categories during model testing. The segment highlights the importance of ensuring that the training set includes all possible categories to avoid errors when new data is introduced.

children.title: Modeling New Majors with OneHotEncoder
children.summary: The discussion highlights the limitation of OneHotEncoder in handling unseen categories, such as a new major introduced by Berkeley. When fitting data, if a category is not present in the training data, predictions cannot be made for that category, leading to a ValueError when attempting to transform new data that includes this unseen category.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's not unique and not a valid value. Some more important if I were trying to let's say model majors and I have like all the majors that I've seen in my training data and then Berkeley creates new major and I try to make prediction. I don't have that major when I fit the data I can't make a prediction for it. Video: 1: File Edit View Run Kernel Tabs Settings Help

children.title: One-Hot Encoding for Categorical Data in Regression
children.summary: One-hot encoding is essential for converting non-numeric data, such as days of the week, into a format suitable for model fitting. The OneHotEncoder class from sklearn is introduced as a method for this process. The discussion includes how to apply one-hot encoding in linear regression, specifically how to merge transformed columns with the original dataset to assess the impact of different days on tips. Notably, Fridays and Sundays are identified as having a higher positive predictive power for tips. An example illustrates the OneHotEncoder's functionality, converting the 'day' feature into four numeric features representing Thursday through Sunday.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding with Sklearn
children.summary: One-hot encoding is necessary for non-numeric data, such as days, before fitting a model. The OneHotEncoder class from sklearn provides a straightforward method for this process. The lecture will explore how to utilize this class effectively.

Because day is non-numeric, we will apply one-hot encoding before fitting a model.
The OneHotEncoder class of sklearn (documentation) offers a quick way to perform one-hot encoding. You will explore its use in
detail in Lab 7. For now, recognize that we follow a very similar workflow to when we were working with the LinearRegression class:
we initialize a OneHotEncoder object, fit it to our data, then use . transform to apply the fitted encoder.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding for Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of one-hot encoding for categorical variables in linear regression. It highlights how to join transformed columns back with the original data to analyze the effect of different days of the week on tips. The speaker notes that Fridays and Sundays show a higher positive predictive power for tips. The OneHotEncoder class from sklearn is introduced as a tool for performing one-hot encoding, with a brief explanation of its initialization, fitting, and transformation process. An example demonstrates how the OneHotEncoder converts the 'day' feature into four numeric features corresponding to the days Thursday through Sunday.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I can just transfer my data. I can join my transformed columns back with my original data and have my matrix just as I'd like it to do my linear regression. So you can feel linear model here. And I get coefficients and each of these coefficients tells me something about The effect of that day of the week on the resulting tip. What might I conclude here? Fridays and Sundays seem to have higher positive predictive power have higher effect on my tip. Good days to tip. Alright, bonus features not only, you know, future final. matrix only includes numeric values.

You can use the scikit learn Pipeline class to define sophisticated feature engineering pipelines that combine different Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
that are all fit at once.
A pipeline is a sequence of transformations and models that are composed to form a new model. It is implemented as a list of ("name",
model/transformation) tuples. Here we have a feature engineering stage followed by a linear model. The feature engineering stage uses a
ColumnTransformer that applies named transformations to selected columns.
[10]:
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn. compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
pl = Pipeline ( [
("Feature Engineering",
ColumnTransformer(
[("OHE", OneHotEncoder(), ["day"] ),
("Polynomial", PolynomialFeatures(4), ['total_bill' ] )],
remainder="passthrough",
force_int_remainder_cols=False)),
("Linear Model", LinearRegression( )) # Linear Model
pl

You can use the scikit learn Pipeline class to define sophisticated feature engineering pipelines that combine different Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
that are all fit at once.
A pipeline is a sequence of transformations and models that are composed to form a new model. It is implemented as a list of ("name",
model/transformation) tuples. Here we have a feature engineering stage followed by a linear model. The feature engineering stage uses a
ColumnTransformer that applies named transformations to selected columns.
[10]:
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn. compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
pl = Pipeline ( [
("Feature Engineering",
ColumnTransformer(
[("OHE", OneHotEncoder(), ["day"] ),
("Polynomial", PolynomialFeatures(4), ['total_bill' ] )],
remainder="passthrough",
force_int_remainder_cols=False)),
("Linear Model", LinearRegression( )) # Linear Model
pl

children.title: Using scikit-learn Pipeline for Feature Engineering and Regression
children.summary: The scikit-learn Pipeline class enables the creation of feature engineering pipelines that combine transformations and models into a single sequence. This sequence consists of ('name', model/transformation) tuples, allowing for simultaneous fitting of the entire pipeline. An example includes using ColumnTransformer for one-hot encoding and polynomial feature generation, followed by a Linear Regression model. This method enhances model expressiveness and simplifies debugging, showcasing how to integrate OneHotEncoder for categorical features and PolynomialFeatures for numerical transformations into a cohesive machine learning workflow.

children.title: Using scikit-learn Pipeline for Feature Engineering
children.summary: The scikit-learn Pipeline class allows for the creation of comprehensive feature engineering pipelines that combine various transformations and models into a single sequence. This sequence is defined as a list of ('name', model/transformation) tuples, facilitating the fitting of the entire pipeline at once. An example includes a feature engineering stage using ColumnTransformer for one-hot encoding and polynomial feature generation, followed by a linear regression model. This approach enhances model expressiveness and simplifies debugging.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: A psychic learn makes it easy to actually compose various feature or transformations into one giant pipeline that you can fit the entire pipeline all at once and then say predict and it does all of this stuff for you. And it manages it keeps track of it, draws cute pictures of everything that's happening. If you get into modeling, this is something you should learn about. It'll make your models much more expressive. It's easier to debug in a future. Video: 1: File Edit View Run Kernel Tabs Settings Help

You can use the scikit learn Pipeline class to define sophisticated feature engineering pipelines that combine different Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
that are all fit at once.
A pipeline is a sequence of transformations and models that are composed to form a new model. It is implemented as a list of ("name",
model/transformation) tuples. Here we have a feature engineering stage followed by a linear model. The feature engineering stage uses a
ColumnTransformer that applies named transformations to selected columns.
[10]:
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn. compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
pl = Pipeline ( [
("Feature Engineering",
ColumnTransformer(
[("OHE", OneHotEncoder(), ["day"] ),
("Polynomial", PolynomialFeatures(4), ['total_bill' ] )],
remainder="passthrough",
force_int_remainder_cols=False)),
("Linear Model", LinearRegression( )) # Linear Model

children.title: Pipeline for Feature Engineering and Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the construction of a machine learning pipeline using Python's sklearn library. It includes the use of ColumnTransformer for feature engineering, applying OneHotEncoder for categorical features and PolynomialFeatures for polynomial transformations of numerical features. The pipeline concludes with a Linear Regression model, demonstrating how to integrate these components into a cohesive workflow.

children.title: Regression Model for Tip Prediction and One-Hot Encoding
children.summary: The segment discusses a regression model predicting tips based on total bill, party size, and day of the week, utilizing one-hot encoding. Coefficients for total_bill and days of the week are provided, with an example calculation for a party of size 3 with a $50 bill on a Friday. The implications of excluding an intercept term in one-hot encoded models are explained, highlighting multicollinearity and the need for full column rank. An example illustrates how coefficients influence predictions, particularly the effect of the day of the week on tip size. The rank of a matrix with a column of ones is examined, showing its impact on invertibility and the redundancy of the intercept term. Additionally, regularization techniques are discussed as a solution to bias issues in models with one-hot encoding, emphasizing the importance of omitting one column or excluding the intercept term to avoid linear dependence.

children.title: Regression Model for Tip Prediction
children.summary: The segment covers a regression model predicting tips based on total bill, party size, and day of the week using one-hot encoding. Coefficients are provided: total_bill (0.092994), day_Fri (0.187132), day_Sat (0.745787), day_Sun (0.621129), and day_Thur (0.668294). An example calculation is presented for a party of size 3 with a total bill of $50 on a Friday, demonstrating how to apply the coefficients in the prediction formula.

children.title: Regression Model Coefficients and Predictions
children.summary: The segment discusses the application of a regression model using one-hot encoding to predict tips based on various features: total bill, party size, and day of the week. The coefficients for each feature are provided: total_bill (0.092994), day_Fri (0.187132), day_Sat (0.745787), day_Sun (0.621129), and day_Thur (0.668294). An example is presented to calculate the predicted tip for a party of size 3 with a total bill of $50 on a Friday, using the formula y = 0.093*50 + 0.187*3 + coefficients for the respective days.

children.title: Predicting Tips Using Regression Model
children.summary: A regression model is presented to predict the tip amount based on three features: total bill, party size, and the day of the week. The coefficients are provided for each feature, indicating their impact on the predicted tip. For example, the model predicts the tip for a party of size 3 with a total bill of $50 on a Friday, using the formula that incorporates coefficients for total_bill, size, and the day of the week.

children.title: Tip Prediction Model Coefficients and Features
children.summary: The segment discusses the coefficients of a model predicting tips based on various features, including total bill, party size, and the day of the week. It highlights that the coefficient for the total bill is approximately 0.093, for party size is about 0.187, and provides specific coefficients for each day of the week: Friday (0.745), Saturday (0.621), Sunday (0.732), and Thursday (0.668). The interpretation emphasizes how the day of the week influences the predicted tip amount.

children.title: Predicting Tips Based on Variables
children.summary: A model for predicting tips based on various factors is discussed, specifically for a party of size 3 with a total bill of $50 on a Friday. The formula used is 0.093*total_bill + 0.187*size + coefficients for the day of the week. The coefficients are detailed: 0.093 for total bill, 0.187 for party size, and varying coefficients for different days of the week, indicating how each factor influences the predicted tip.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding and Intercept Term Implications
children.summary: The discussion covers the exclusion of an intercept term in one-hot encoded models, explaining that this is due to multicollinearity and the need to maintain full column rank. It illustrates how one-hot encoding creates a column of 1s that acts as a fixed intercept, leading to linear dependence among features. An example is provided to calculate predicted tips based on total bill, party size, and day of the week, emphasizing how coefficients for each feature influence predictions. The significance of model coefficients is interpreted, particularly regarding the impact of the day of the week on predicted tip size. Additionally, the rank of a matrix formed by adding a column of ones is examined, revealing that the matrix does not have full column rank, which affects its invertibility and highlights the redundancy of the intercept term in one-hot encoded models.

children.title: Exclusion of Intercept in One-Hot Models
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the rationale for not including an intercept term in one-hot encoded models. It highlights the implications for linear models, particularly regarding the sum of one-hot encoded features. An example is provided, calculating predicted tips based on total bill, party size, and day of the week, demonstrating how each feature's coefficient influences the prediction. The significance of the model coefficients for each feature is also interpreted, particularly how the day of the week affects the predicted tip size.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding and Intercept Term Issues
children.summary: Discussion on why an intercept term is not included in one-hot encoded models due to issues of multicollinearity and maintaining full column rank. It was explained that one-hot encoding creates a column of 1s, which acts as a fixed intercept, leading to linear dependence among features. The sum of one-hot encoded features should equal 1, which can introduce bias and affect model accuracy. Examples were provided to illustrate how the presence of too many features can skew data and create unreasonable results.

children.title: Matrix Rank and Intercept Term in Linear Models
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the rank of a matrix formed by adding a column of ones to represent the intercept term in a linear model. It is explained that the linear combination of three columns results in a column of all ones, indicating that the matrix does not have full column rank. The rank is determined to be 3 rather than 4 due to linear dependence among the columns. The implications of this rank on the invertibility of the matrix are highlighted, noting that the intercept term is already represented in the one-hot encoded columns, which can lead to issues in matrix inversion.

children.title: Regularization and One-Hot Encoding in Linear Models
children.summary: Regularization techniques can mitigate issues with bias terms in models, particularly when using one-hot encoded columns. Including a bias term with one-hot encoding leads to linear dependence in the design matrix, rendering it non-invertible and causing failures in ordinary least squares (OLS) estimates. To resolve this, one can omit one of the one-hot encoded columns or not include an intercept term. Additionally, polynomial features can be introduced by squaring variables to handle non-linearity while maintaining a linear combination of features.

children.title: Regularization and One-Hot Encoding Issues
children.summary: Regularization techniques can address issues related to bias terms in models. It is crucial to avoid including a bias term when using one-hot encoded columns, as this leads to linear dependence in the design matrix, making it non-invertible and causing failures in ordinary least squares (OLS) estimates. To resolve this, one can omit one of the one-hot encoded columns, not include an intercept term, or apply regularization.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding and Linear Models
children.summary: Discusses the importance of properly applying one-hot encoding in linear models, highlighting that including a bias column with one-hot encoded columns leads to linear dependence, making the design matrix non-invertible. To resolve this, one can either omit one of the one-hot encoded columns or not include an intercept term. Additionally, polynomial features can be introduced by squaring variables to manage non-linearity, while still maintaining a linear combination of features.

children.title: Increasing Model Complexity with Polynomial Terms
children.summary: The discussion focuses on enhancing model complexity by adding polynomial terms to improve data fitting. Introducing more features increases the model's ability to minimize error and achieve better fits. However, models often operate on samples rather than the complete dataset, leading to potential overfitting. A polynomial may perfectly fit a sample with zero error, but this does not guarantee a good fit for the entire dataset, emphasizing the importance of understanding data limitations.

children.title: Increasing Model Complexity with Polynomial Terms
children.summary: The discussion focuses on enhancing model complexity by adding polynomial terms to improve data fitting. As more features are introduced, the model's ability to minimize error increases, leading to better fits. However, a key concern is that in practice, models often operate on samples rather than the complete dataset. While a polynomial can perfectly fit a sample with zero error, this may not translate to a good fit when applied to the entire dataset, highlighting the challenges of overfitting and the importance of understanding the limitations of the data available.

Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...

· ·

high variance means they can't be reapplied to new datasets

children.title: Error, Variance, and Model Complexity Trade-off
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the trade-off between training error and model complexity. It explains how increasing model complexity can decrease training error, but if the model becomes too complex, it may overfit the data, leading to high variance and poor generalization to new datasets. Key concepts covered include underfitting, overfitting, training error, and the effects of model complexity, particularly regarding the number of features.

children.title: Error, Variance, and Model Complexity
children.summary: The discussion addresses the trade-off between training error and model complexity. Increasing model complexity can reduce training error, but overly complex models may overfit the data, resulting in high variance and poor generalization to new datasets. Key concepts include underfitting, overfitting, training error, and the impact of model complexity, such as the number of features.
